content:
You are an LLVM compiler expert specializing in test case generation and bug detection. You are conducting LLVM code review now.

## Context
Maintainers provided a patch, but patches often have edge cases or introduce new issues.

## Your Task
You will work in two phases:
- Phase 1: Analyze the fix to identify potential issues, gaps, and edge cases. Propose test strategies.
- Phase 2: Generate targeted test cases by modifying existing tests to target identified issues

## You Will Receive
- Original patch description and related test case(s)
- The code region that was modified (the patch)
- Information about the optimization pass and component involved

## Tools You Can Use

You have multiple tools for each phase, but you should try to avoid using them with same inputs repeatedly to reduce redundant calls.

**CRITICAL**: You MUST use tool call every action in Phase 1 and Phase 2.

file: llvm/include/llvm/Analysis/ValueTracking.h:680-930
--------------------------------------------------------
680 LLVM_ABI ConstantRange getVScaleRange(const Function *F, unsigned BitWidth);
681 
682 /// Determine the possible constant range of an integer or vector of integer
683 /// value. This is intended as a cheap, non-recursive check.
684 LLVM_ABI ConstantRange computeConstantRange(const Value *V, bool ForSigned,
685                                             bool UseInstrInfo = true,
686                                             AssumptionCache *AC = nullptr,
687                                             const Instruction *CtxI = nullptr,
688                                             const DominatorTree *DT = nullptr,
689                                             unsigned Depth = 0);
690 
691 /// Combine constant ranges from computeConstantRange() and computeKnownBits().
692 LLVM_ABI ConstantRange computeConstantRangeIncludingKnownBits(
693     const WithCache<const Value *> &V, bool ForSigned, const SimplifyQuery &SQ);
694 
695 /// Return true if this function can prove that the instruction I will
696 /// always transfer execution to one of its successors (including the next
697 /// instruction that follows within a basic block). E.g. this is not
698 /// guaranteed for function calls that could loop infinitely.
699 ///
700 /// In other words, this function returns false for instructions that may
701 /// transfer execution or fail to transfer execution in a way that is not
702 /// captured in the CFG nor in the sequence of instructions within a basic
703 /// block.
704 ///
705 /// Undefined behavior is assumed not to happen, so e.g. division is
706 /// guaranteed to transfer execution to the following instruction even
707 /// though division by zero might cause undefined behavior.
708 LLVM_ABI bool isGuaranteedToTransferExecutionToSuccessor(const Instruction *I);
709 
710 /// Returns true if this block does not contain a potential implicit exit.
711 /// This is equivelent to saying that all instructions within the basic block
712 /// are guaranteed to transfer execution to their successor within the basic
713 /// block. This has the same assumptions w.r.t. undefined behavior as the
714 /// instruction variant of this function.
715 LLVM_ABI bool isGuaranteedToTransferExecutionToSuccessor(const BasicBlock *BB);
716 
717 /// Return true if every instruction in the range (Begin, End) is
718 /// guaranteed to transfer execution to its static successor. \p ScanLimit
719 /// bounds the search to avoid scanning huge blocks.
720 LLVM_ABI bool
721 isGuaranteedToTransferExecutionToSuccessor(BasicBlock::const_iterator Begin,
722                                            BasicBlock::const_iterator End,
723                                            unsigned ScanLimit = 32);
724 
725 /// Same as previous, but with range expressed via iterator_range.
726 LLVM_ABI bool isGuaranteedToTransferExecutionToSuccessor(
727     iterator_range<BasicBlock::const_iterator> Range, unsigned ScanLimit = 32);
728 
729 /// Return true if this function can prove that the instruction I
730 /// is executed for every iteration of the loop L.
731 ///
732 /// Note that this currently only considers the loop header.
733 LLVM_ABI bool isGuaranteedToExecuteForEveryIteration(const Instruction *I,
734                                                      const Loop *L);
735 
736 /// Return true if \p PoisonOp's user yields poison or raises UB if its
737 /// operand \p PoisonOp is poison.
738 ///
739 /// If \p PoisonOp is a vector or an aggregate and the operation's result is a
740 /// single value, any poison element in /p PoisonOp should make the result
741 /// poison or raise UB.
742 ///
743 /// To filter out operands that raise UB on poison, you can use
744 /// getGuaranteedNonPoisonOp.
745 LLVM_ABI bool propagatesPoison(const Use &PoisonOp);
746 
747 /// Return whether this intrinsic propagates poison for all operands.
748 LLVM_ABI bool intrinsicPropagatesPoison(Intrinsic::ID IID);
749 
750 /// Return true if the given instruction must trigger undefined behavior
751 /// when I is executed with any operands which appear in KnownPoison holding
752 /// a poison value at the point of execution.
753 LLVM_ABI bool mustTriggerUB(const Instruction *I,
754                             const SmallPtrSetImpl<const Value *> &KnownPoison);
755 
756 /// Return true if this function can prove that if Inst is executed
757 /// and yields a poison value or undef bits, then that will trigger
758 /// undefined behavior.
759 ///
760 /// Note that this currently only considers the basic block that is
761 /// the parent of Inst.
762 LLVM_ABI bool programUndefinedIfUndefOrPoison(const Instruction *Inst);
763 LLVM_ABI bool programUndefinedIfPoison(const Instruction *Inst);
764 
765 /// canCreateUndefOrPoison returns true if Op can create undef or poison from
766 /// non-undef & non-poison operands.
767 /// For vectors, canCreateUndefOrPoison returns true if there is potential
768 /// poison or undef in any element of the result when vectors without
769 /// undef/poison poison are given as operands.
770 /// For example, given `Op = shl <2 x i32> %x, <0, 32>`, this function returns
771 /// true. If Op raises immediate UB but never creates poison or undef
772 /// (e.g. sdiv I, 0), canCreatePoison returns false.
773 ///
774 /// \p ConsiderFlagsAndMetadata controls whether poison producing flags and
775 /// metadata on the instruction are considered.  This can be used to see if the
776 /// instruction could still introduce undef or poison even without poison
777 /// generating flags and metadata which might be on the instruction.
778 /// (i.e. could the result of Op->dropPoisonGeneratingFlags() still create
779 /// poison or undef)
780 ///
781 /// canCreatePoison returns true if Op can create poison from non-poison
782 /// operands.
783 LLVM_ABI bool canCreateUndefOrPoison(const Operator *Op,
784                                      bool ConsiderFlagsAndMetadata = true);
785 LLVM_ABI bool canCreatePoison(const Operator *Op,
786                               bool ConsiderFlagsAndMetadata = true);
787 
788 /// Return true if V is poison given that ValAssumedPoison is already poison.
789 /// For example, if ValAssumedPoison is `icmp X, 10` and V is `icmp X, 5`,
790 /// impliesPoison returns true.
791 LLVM_ABI bool impliesPoison(const Value *ValAssumedPoison, const Value *V);
792 
793 /// Return true if this function can prove that V does not have undef bits
794 /// and is never poison. If V is an aggregate value or vector, check whether
795 /// all elements (except padding) are not undef or poison.
796 /// Note that this is different from canCreateUndefOrPoison because the
797 /// function assumes Op's operands are not poison/undef.
798 ///
799 /// If CtxI and DT are specified this method performs flow-sensitive analysis
800 /// and returns true if it is guaranteed to be never undef or poison
801 /// immediately before the CtxI.
802 LLVM_ABI bool
803 isGuaranteedNotToBeUndefOrPoison(const Value *V, AssumptionCache *AC = nullptr,
804                                  const Instruction *CtxI = nullptr,
805                                  const DominatorTree *DT = nullptr,
806                                  unsigned Depth = 0);
807 
808 /// Returns true if V cannot be poison, but may be undef.
809 LLVM_ABI bool isGuaranteedNotToBePoison(const Value *V,
810                                         AssumptionCache *AC = nullptr,
811                                         const Instruction *CtxI = nullptr,
812                                         const DominatorTree *DT = nullptr,
813                                         unsigned Depth = 0);
814 
815 inline bool isGuaranteedNotToBePoison(const Value *V, AssumptionCache *AC,
816                                       BasicBlock::iterator CtxI,
817                                       const DominatorTree *DT = nullptr,
818                                       unsigned Depth = 0) {
819   // Takes an iterator as a position, passes down to Instruction *
820   // implementation.
821   return isGuaranteedNotToBePoison(V, AC, &*CtxI, DT, Depth);
822 }
823 
824 /// Returns true if V cannot be undef, but may be poison.
825 LLVM_ABI bool isGuaranteedNotToBeUndef(const Value *V,
826                                        AssumptionCache *AC = nullptr,
827                                        const Instruction *CtxI = nullptr,
828                                        const DominatorTree *DT = nullptr,
829                                        unsigned Depth = 0);
830 
831 /// Return true if undefined behavior would provable be executed on the path to
832 /// OnPathTo if Root produced a posion result.  Note that this doesn't say
833 /// anything about whether OnPathTo is actually executed or whether Root is
834 /// actually poison.  This can be used to assess whether a new use of Root can
835 /// be added at a location which is control equivalent with OnPathTo (such as
836 /// immediately before it) without introducing UB which didn't previously
837 /// exist.  Note that a false result conveys no information.
838 LLVM_ABI bool mustExecuteUBIfPoisonOnPathTo(Instruction *Root,
839                                             Instruction *OnPathTo,
840                                             DominatorTree *DT);
841 
842 /// Convert an integer comparison with a constant RHS into an equivalent
843 /// form with the strictness flipped predicate. Return the new predicate and
844 /// corresponding constant RHS if possible. Otherwise return std::nullopt.
845 /// E.g., (icmp sgt X, 0) -> (icmp sle X, 1).
846 LLVM_ABI std::optional<std::pair<CmpPredicate, Constant *>>
847 getFlippedStrictnessPredicateAndConstant(CmpPredicate Pred, Constant *C);
848 
849 /// Specific patterns of select instructions we can match.
850 enum SelectPatternFlavor {
851   SPF_UNKNOWN = 0,
852   SPF_SMIN,    /// Signed minimum
853   SPF_UMIN,    /// Unsigned minimum
854   SPF_SMAX,    /// Signed maximum
855   SPF_UMAX,    /// Unsigned maximum
856   SPF_FMINNUM, /// Floating point minnum
857   SPF_FMAXNUM, /// Floating point maxnum
858   SPF_ABS,     /// Absolute value
859   SPF_NABS     /// Negated absolute value
860 };
861 
862 /// Behavior when a floating point min/max is given one NaN and one
863 /// non-NaN as input.
864 enum SelectPatternNaNBehavior {
865   SPNB_NA = 0,        /// NaN behavior not applicable.
866   SPNB_RETURNS_NAN,   /// Given one NaN input, returns the NaN.
867   SPNB_RETURNS_OTHER, /// Given one NaN input, returns the non-NaN.
868   SPNB_RETURNS_ANY    /// Given one NaN input, can return either (or
869                       /// it has been determined that no operands can
870                       /// be NaN).
871 };
872 
873 struct SelectPatternResult {
874   SelectPatternFlavor Flavor;
875   SelectPatternNaNBehavior NaNBehavior; /// Only applicable if Flavor is
876                                         /// SPF_FMINNUM or SPF_FMAXNUM.
877   bool Ordered; /// When implementing this min/max pattern as
878                 /// fcmp; select, does the fcmp have to be
879                 /// ordered?
880 
881   /// Return true if \p SPF is a min or a max pattern.
882   static bool isMinOrMax(SelectPatternFlavor SPF) {
883     return SPF != SPF_UNKNOWN && SPF != SPF_ABS && SPF != SPF_NABS;
884   }
885 };
886 
887 /// Pattern match integer [SU]MIN, [SU]MAX and ABS idioms, returning the kind
888 /// and providing the out parameter results if we successfully match.
889 ///
890 /// For ABS/NABS, LHS will be set to the input to the abs idiom. RHS will be
891 /// the negation instruction from the idiom.
892 ///
893 /// If CastOp is not nullptr, also match MIN/MAX idioms where the type does
894 /// not match that of the original select. If this is the case, the cast
895 /// operation (one of Trunc,SExt,Zext) that must be done to transform the
896 /// type of LHS and RHS into the type of V is returned in CastOp.
897 ///
898 /// For example:
899 ///   %1 = icmp slt i32 %a, i32 4
900 ///   %2 = sext i32 %a to i64
901 ///   %3 = select i1 %1, i64 %2, i64 4
902 ///
903 /// -> LHS = %a, RHS = i32 4, *CastOp = Instruction::SExt
904 ///
905 LLVM_ABI SelectPatternResult
906 matchSelectPattern(Value *V, Value *&LHS, Value *&RHS,
907                    Instruction::CastOps *CastOp = nullptr, unsigned Depth = 0);
908 
909 inline SelectPatternResult matchSelectPattern(const Value *V, const Value *&LHS,
910                                               const Value *&RHS) {
911   Value *L = const_cast<Value *>(LHS);
912   Value *R = const_cast<Value *>(RHS);
913   auto Result = matchSelectPattern(const_cast<Value *>(V), L, R);
914   LHS = L;
915   RHS = R;
916   return Result;
917 }
918 
919 /// Determine the pattern that a select with the given compare as its
920 /// predicate and given values as its true/false operands would match.
921 LLVM_ABI SelectPatternResult matchDecomposedSelectPattern(
922     CmpInst *CmpI, Value *TrueVal, Value *FalseVal, Value *&LHS, Value *&RHS,
923     FastMathFlags FMF = FastMathFlags(), Instruction::CastOps *CastOp = nullptr,
924     unsigned Depth = 0);
925 
926 /// Determine the pattern for predicate `X Pred Y ? X : Y`.
927 LLVM_ABI SelectPatternResult getSelectPattern(
928     CmpInst::Predicate Pred, SelectPatternNaNBehavior NaNBehavior = SPNB_NA,
929     bool Ordered = false);
--------------------------------------------------------

name: langref
arguments: {"inst":"load"}

output:
.. _i_load:

'``load``' Instruction
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

Syntax:
"""""""

::

      <result> = load [volatile] <ty>, ptr <pointer>[, align <alignment>][, !nontemporal !<nontemp_node>][, !invariant.load !<empty_node>][, !invariant.group !<empty_node>][, !nonnull !<empty_node>][, !dereferenceable !<deref_bytes_node>][, !dereferenceable_or_null !<deref_bytes_node>][, !align !<align_node>][, !noundef !<empty_node>]
      <result> = load atomic [volatile] <ty>, ptr <pointer> [syncscope("<target-scope>")] <ordering>, align <alignment> [, !invariant.group !<empty_node>]
      !<nontemp_node> = !{ i32 1 }
      !<empty_node> = !{}
      !<deref_bytes_node> = !{ i64 <dereferenceable_bytes> }
      !<align_node> = !{ i64 <value_alignment> }

Overview:
"""""""""

The '``load``' instruction is used to read from memory.

Arguments:
""""""""""

The argument to the ``load`` instruction specifies the memory address from which
to load. The type specified must be a :ref:`first class <t_firstclass>` type of
known size (i.e., not containing an :ref:`opaque structural type <t_opaque>`). If
the ``load`` is marked as ``volatile``, then the optimizer is not allowed to
modify the number or order of execution of this ``load`` with other
:ref:`volatile operations <volatile>`.

If the ``load`` is marked as ``atomic``, it takes an extra :ref:`ordering
<ordering>` and optional ``syncscope("<target-scope>")`` argument. The
``release`` and ``acq_rel`` orderings are not valid on ``load`` instructions.
Atomic loads produce :ref:`defined <memmodel>` results when they may see
multiple atomic stores. The type of the pointee must be an integer, pointer,
floating-point, or vector type whose bit width is a power of two greater than
or equal to eight. ``align`` must be
explicitly specified on atomic loads. Note: if the alignment is not greater or
equal to the size of the `<value>` type, the atomic operation is likely to
require a lock and have poor performance. ``!nontemporal`` does not have any
defined semantics for atomic loads.

The optional constant ``align`` argument specifies the alignment of the
operation (that is, the alignment of the memory address). It is the
responsibility of the code emitter to ensure that the alignment information is
correct. Overestimating the alignment results in undefined behavior.
Underestimating the alignment may produce less efficient code. An alignment of
1 is always safe. The maximum possible alignment is ``1 << 32``. An alignment
value higher than the size of the loaded type does *not* imply (without target
specific knowledge) that memory up to the alignment value bytes can be safely
loaded without trapping.

The alignment is only optional when parsing textual IR; for in-memory IR, it is
always present. An omitted ``align`` argument means that the operation has the
ABI alignment for the target.

The optional ``!nontemporal`` metadata must reference a single
metadata name ``<nontemp_node>`` corresponding to a metadata node with one
``i32`` entry of value 1. The existence of the ``!nontemporal``
metadata on the instruction tells the optimizer and code generator
that this load is not expected to be reused in the cache. The code
generator may select special instructions to save cache bandwidth, such
as the ``MOVNT`` instruction on x86.

The optional ``!invariant.load`` metadata must reference a single
metadata name ``<empty_node>`` corresponding to a metadata node with no
entries. If a load instruction tagged with the ``!invariant.load``
metadata is executed, the memory location referenced by the load has
to contain the same value at all points in the program where the
memory location is dereferenceable; otherwise, the behavior is
undefined.

The optional ``!invariant.group`` metadata must reference a single metadata name
 ``<empty_node>`` corresponding to a metadata node with no entries.
 See ``invariant.group`` metadata :ref:`invariant.group <md_invariant.group>`.

The optional ``!nonnull`` metadata must reference a single
metadata name ``<empty_node>`` corresponding to a metadata node with no
entries. The existence of the ``!nonnull`` metadata on the
instruction tells the optimizer that the value loaded is known to
never be null. If the value is null at runtime, a poison value is returned
instead.  This is analogous to the ``nonnull`` attribute on parameters and
return values. This metadata can only be applied to loads of a pointer type.

The optional ``!dereferenceable`` metadata must reference a single metadata
name ``<deref_bytes_node>`` corresponding to a metadata node with one ``i64``
entry.
See ``dereferenceable`` metadata :ref:`dereferenceable <md_dereferenceable>`.

The optional ``!dereferenceable_or_null`` metadata must reference a single
metadata name ``<deref_bytes_node>`` corresponding to a metadata node with one
``i64`` entry.
See ``dereferenceable_or_null`` metadata :ref:`dereferenceable_or_null
<md_dereferenceable_or_null>`.

The optional ``!align`` metadata must reference a single metadata name
``<align_node>`` corresponding to a metadata node with one ``i64`` entry.
The existence of the ``!align`` metadata on the instruction tells the
optimizer that the value loaded is known to be aligned to a boundary specified
by the integer value in the metadata node. The alignment must be a power of 2.
This is analogous to the ''align'' attribute on parameters and return values.
This metadata can only be applied to loads of a pointer type. If the returned
value is not appropriately aligned at runtime, a poison value is returned
instead.

The optional ``!noundef`` metadata must reference a single metadata name
``<empty_node>`` corresponding to a node with no entries. The existence of
``!noundef`` metadata on the instruction tells the optimizer that the value
loaded is known to be :ref:`well defined <welldefinedvalues>`.
If the value isn't well defined, the behavior is undefined. If the ``!noundef``
metadata is combined with poison-generating metadata like ``!nonnull``,
violation of that metadata constraint will also result in undefined behavior.

Semantics:
""""""""""

The location of memory pointed to is loaded. If the value being loaded
is of scalar type then the number of bytes read does not exceed the
minimum number of bytes needed to hold all bits of the type. For
example, loading an ``i24`` reads at most three bytes. When loading a
value of a type like ``i20`` with a size that is not an integral number
of bytes, the result is undefined if the value was not originally
written using a store of the same type.
If the value being loaded is of aggregate type, the bytes that correspond to
padding may be accessed but are ignored, because it is impossible to observe
padding from the loaded aggregate value.
If ``<pointer>`` is not a well-defined value, the behavior is undefined.

Examples:
"""""""""

.. code-block:: llvm

      %ptr = alloca i32                               ; yields ptr
      store i32 3, ptr %ptr                           ; yields void
      %val = load i32, ptr %ptr                       ; yields i32:val = i32 3

If `SubI` is `%add = add nuw i32 %x, 1`, it decomposes to `%x + 1 >= UMin`.
This constrains `%x`!
And `%x` is defined *before* `SubI` (and potentially before the check).
So a check *before* `SubI` could be simplified using the fact from `SubI`!
Let's construct a concrete example.
```llvm
define i1 @test(i32 %x) {
entry:
  ; Check before SubI
  %cmp1 = icmp uge i32 %x, 0  ; Can this be simplified?
  
  ; SubI
  %add = add nu

arguments:
{"thoughts":"
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 1: Prioritize specific tools.
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 2: List related tools.

1.  **Fix Understanding**:
    The patch modifies `ConstraintElimination` to use `computeConstantRange` to extract facts from the operands of comparisons.
    Specifically, for any `ICmpInst` that is not an equality comparison, it looks at its operands (`OpI`) and the operands of those operands (`SubI`).
    For each such instruction, it calls `computeConstantRange` (with `UseInstrInfo=true`, which means it will use `nuw`/`nsw` flags, `assume`s, etc.).
    If the computed constant range is not full or empty, it extracts the unsigned minimum and maximum.
    It then adds `ICMP_UGE V, UMin` and `ICMP_ULE V, UMax` as `ConditionFact`s to the constraint system.
    Crucially, these facts are added at `DT.getNode(OpI->getParent())` (or `SubI->getParent()`).

2.  **Formal Semantic Modeling**:
    Before the fix: `ConstraintElimination` only extracted facts from branches, assumes, and some specific intrinsics.
    After the fix: It also extracts facts from the *results* of instructions that feed into comparisons.
    The fact added is: `V >= UMin` and `V <= UMax`, where `UMin` and `UMax` are derived from `computeConstantRange(V)`.
    This fact is added at the dominator tree node of `V->getParent()`.
    Because it's added as a `ConditionFact`, `stable_sort` will place it *before* any `InstCheck` or `UseCheck` in the same basic block.

3.  **Assumptions Identified**:
    - **Assumption 1**: The constant range computed by `computeConstantRange` is valid for all executions of the block `V->getParent()`.
      - *Violation*: `computeConstantRange` with `UseInstrInfo=true` uses poison-generating flags like `nuw` and `nsw`. If `V` is `add nuw x, y`, its range assumes no overflow. If overflow occurs, `V` is poison.
      - If `V` is poison, the fact `V >= UMin` is technically evaluating poison, which is poison. But `ConstraintElimination` uses this fact to constrain `x` and `y`!
      - Wait, `ConstraintElimination` decomposes `V >= UMin`. If `V` is `add nuw x, y`, it decomposes to `x + y >= UMin`. This constrains `x` and `y` as if the addition did not overflow.
      - Is it valid to assume the addition did not overflow for *other* instructions in the same block or dominated blocks?
      - NO! If the addition overflows, `V` is poison. If `V` is only used in a `select` or a `phi` and never causes UB, the program is well-defined. But we used the assumption that it didn't overflow to simplify *another* check. This is a miscompilation!
    - **Assumption 2**: The fact is valid for all instructions in `V->getParent()`.
      - *Violation*: Even if `V` *does* cause UB when it's poison (e.g., it's used in a branch), the UB only happens *after* `V` is executed.
      - If there is a check *before* `V` in the same block, we cannot use the fact from `V` to simplify the check, because the program might exit or loop before reaching `V`.
      - Since `ConditionFact`s are sorted before checks in the same block, a fact from `V` at the end of the block will be used to simplify a check at the beginning of the block.

4.  **Potential Cases to Break Assumptions**:
    - **Case 1**: `V` is an instruction with `nuw`/`nsw` flags. It is used in a comparison, which is used in a `select` (so no UB if poison). Another comparison in the same block (or a dominated block) checks a condition that can be simplified if `V`'s `nuw`/`nsw` holds. The pass will simplify the second comparison, which is wrong if `V` actually overflows.
    - **Case 2**: `V` is an instruction with `nuw`/`nsw` flags. It is used in a branch (so UB if poison). However, there is another comparison *before* `V` in the same block. The pass simplifies the earlier comparison using the fact from `V`. This is wrong if the block can exit (e.g., via a throwing call or an earlier branch) before reaching `V`.

5.  **Test Strategies**:
    - Strategy 1: Poison-generating instruction used in a non-branching context.
    - Strategy 2: Fact from instruction used to simplify a check before it in the same block.
    - Strategy 3: Fact from instruction in a dominating block used in a dominated block, where the instruction's poison is not observed.
    - Strategy 4: Fact from `SubI` (operand of operand) which is poison-generating and not guaranteed to be observed.
    - Strategy 5: `computeConstantRange` using `assume` that is after the check. (Wait, `computeConstantRange` uses `CtxI`? The patch calls `computeConstantRange(V, false, true)`. It does *not* pass `CtxI` or `AC` or `DT`. So it only uses context-independent facts, like `nuw`/`nsw` flags on the instruction itself, or range metadata). Range metadata on `V` has the same poison semantics: if the value is outside the range, it's poison. So range metadata can also be used to trigger this bug.","strategies":[{"name":"Poison-generating instruction used for constant range fact","rationale":"The patch extracts constant range facts from operands of comparisons. If an operand is an instruction with poison-generating flags (e.g., `add nuw`), `computeConstantRange` might use these flags to compute a tighter range. However, if the comparison itself is not guaranteed to execute (e.g., it's in a different basic block or after a potential exit), or if the fact is applied to a check that dominates the poison-generating instruction, it could lead to incorrect simplifications based on unobserved poison. The patch adds facts at the `DTN` of the instruction's parent block, which means the fact is assumed to hold for all instructions dominated by that block. But if the instruction is not guaranteed to execute within that block (e.g., it's after a call that might not return), or if the fact is used to simplify a check that comes *before* the instruction in the same block, it's invalid. Wait, the patch adds the fact at `DT.getNode(OpI->getParent())`. This means the fact is available for any check dominated by `OpI->getParent()`. If `OpI` is in the same block as a check, but *after* the check, the fact will still be available because `stable_sort` sorts by `NumIn` (which is the same for the whole block) and then by relative order. Wait, `stable_sort` sorts by relative order in the basic block! So facts from `OpI` will come *after* checks that precede `OpI` in the block? Let's check `stable_sort` logic. `stable_sort` puts facts before checks if they have the same `NumIn`? No, it uses `InstA->comesBefore(InstB)`. But `addConstantRangeFact` creates a `ConditionFact` with `Inst` set to `nullptr`? No, `getConditionFact` doesn't set `Inst`. Wait, `getConditionFact` sets `Ty = EntryTy::ConditionFact`. In `stable_sort`, if both are `ConditionFact`, it sorts by `HasNoConstOp`. If one is `ConditionFact` and the other is not, `ConditionFact` comes first! So `ConditionFact`s are processed *before* `InstCheck`s and `UseCheck`s in the same block, regardless of their actual position in the block! This means a fact derived from an instruction at the end of the block will be used to simplify a check at the beginning of the block. If the instruction at the end of the block produces poison (e.g., `add nuw`), and the check at the beginning of the block is simplified based on this, it's a bug if the block can exit before reaching the instruction, or if the instruction's poison is not guaranteed to be observed.","target":"A test case with a comparison, where one of the operands is an instruction with poison-generating flags (like `add nuw` or `shl nuw`) located *after* another comparison in the same block, or in a block that doesn't strictly dominate the check.","expected_issue":"The pass might incorrectly simplify a comparison that executes before the poison-generating instruction, leading to a miscompilation where the comparison result is based on a poison value that hasn't been observed yet."},{"name":"Fact from instruction not guaranteed to execute","expected_issue":"The pass might incorrectly simplify a comparison based on a fact derived from an instruction that is never executed, leading to a miscompilation.","rationale":"The patch extracts constant range facts from operands of comparisons. It iterates over `Cmp->operands()` and their operands, and adds facts at `DT.getNode(OpI->getParent())`. If `OpI` is in a basic block, the fact is added at that block's `DTN`. Because `ConditionFact`s are sorted before checks in the same block, the fact will be applied to all checks in the block. However, `OpI` might not be guaranteed to execute. For example, if there is a function call that might throw or not return before `OpI` in the same block, `OpI` might never execute. If `OpI` has poison-generating flags, `computeConstantRange` might assume those flags hold. But if `OpI` never executes, those flags are irrelevant, and using them to simplify a check before the throwing call is invalid.","target":"A test case with a check, followed by a throwing/non-returning call, followed by a comparison that uses an instruction with poison-generating flags."},{"name":"Fact from conditionally executed instruction in the same block","expected_issue":"The pass might incorrectly simplify a comparison based on a fact derived from an instruction in a different function or an unreachable block, though less likely. More importantly, it might extract facts from instructions that are conditionally executed but add them at the block level.","target":"A test case where an instruction with `nuw`/`nsw` is used in a comparison, and the fact derived from it is used to simplify a check that executes *before* the instruction, or on a path where the instruction's poison is not observed.","rationale":"Wait, `OpI` is an operand of `Cmp`. If `Cmp` is in block `B`, `OpI` must dominate `Cmp`. So `OpI` is either in `B` or in a dominator of `B`. The fact is added at `DT.getNode(OpI->getParent())`. This means the fact is assumed to hold for all blocks dominated by `OpI->getParent()`. This is generally correct *if* the fact is an inherent property of `OpI`'s result. `computeConstantRange` computes the range of `OpI`'s result. If `OpI` is `add nuw x, y`, the result is in a certain range *if* it's not poison. If it is poison, the result is poison, and any use of it is poison. If `Cmp` uses `OpI`, and `Cmp` is reached, then `OpI` was executed. But wait! What if `OpI` is used by `Cmp`, but we use the fact to simplify *another* check that uses `x`? The fact `x + y >= UMin` is derived from `add nuw x, y`. This fact is added at `OpI->getParent()`. It will be used to simplify any check dominated by `OpI->getParent()`. But what if `OpI` is poison? If `OpI` is poison, `x + y >= UMin` might be false. Is it valid to assume `x + y >= UMin` for other checks just because `OpI` exists? No! If `OpI` is poison, it doesn't trigger UB unless it's used in a UB-triggering way. `Cmp` using poison just produces poison, which might branch on poison (UB), but what if `Cmp` is part of a `select` or `phi` and never branched on? Or what if the other check is executed *before* `OpI`? If the other check is executed before `OpI`, and we simplify it based on `OpI`'s `nuw` flag, we are assuming `OpI` is not poison. But `OpI` hasn't even executed yet! This is a classic poison propagation bug."},{"rationale":"If `OpI` is `add nuw x, 1`, and it's used in `Cmp`, and `Cmp` is used in a `select`, then `OpI` being poison just makes the `select` result poison. It doesn't trigger UB. But the patch adds the fact `OpI >= UMin` at `OpI->getParent()`. This fact is then used to simplify other checks in `OpI->getParent()` or dominated blocks. If another check `x < 10` is simplified based on this fact, it might be miscompiled because `OpI` being poison doesn't guarantee `x` satisfies the `nuw` condition. The fact is only valid if `OpI` is guaranteed to not be poison, or if the simplified check is only used when `OpI` is not poison. But the patch adds the fact unconditionally for all dominated checks!","target":"A test case where `OpI` with `nuw`/`nsw` is used in a `icmp`, which is used in a `select` (so no UB if poison). Another `icmp` in the same block or a dominated block is simplified based on the fact from `OpI`.","expected_issue":"The pass might incorrectly simplify a comparison based on a fact derived from an instruction that is not guaranteed to execute, because the fact is added at the instruction's parent block, which might not dominate the check, or the check might be before the instruction.","name":"Poison-generating instruction used in a non-branching context"},{"name":"Fact from instruction in a dominating block used in a dominated block","rationale":"Similar to the above, if `OpI` is in block A, and it's used in a `icmp` in block A, and the `icmp` is used in a `select`. This leads to a miscompilation.","target":"A test case with `OpI` in block A, used in a `select` in block A. The fact is added at `DT.getNode(OpI->getParent())`. This means the fact is assumed to hold for all blocks dominated by `OpI->getParent()`. This is generally correct *if* the fact is an inherent property of `OpI`'s result. `computeConstantRange` computes the range of `OpI`'s result. If `OpI` is `add nuw x, y`, the result is in a certain range *if* it's not poison. If it is poison, the result is poison, and any use of it is poison. If `Cmp` uses `OpI`, and `Cmp` is reached, then `OpI` was executed. But wait! What if `OpI` is used by `Cmp`, but we use the fact to simplify *another* check that uses `x`? The fact `x + y >= UMin` is derived from `add nuw x, y`. This fact is added at `OpI->getParent()`. It will be used to simplify any check dominated by `OpI->getParent()`. But what if `OpI` is poison? If `OpI` is poison, `x + y >= UMin` might be false. Is it valid to assume `x + y >= UMin` for other checks just because `OpI` exists? No! If `OpI` is poison, it doesn't trigger UB unless it's used in a UB-triggering way. `Cmp` using poison just produces poison, which might branch on poison (UB), but what if `Cmp` is part of a `select` or `phi` and never branched on? Or what if the other check is executed *before* `OpI`? If the other check is executed before `OpI`, and we simplify it based on `OpI`'s `nuw` flag, we are assuming `OpI` is not poison. But `OpI` hasn't even executed yet! This is a classic poison propagation bug.",
      "The pass might incorrectly simplify a comparison based on a fact derived from an instruction in a different function or an unreachable block, though less likely. More importantly, it might extract facts from instructions that are conditionally executed but add them at the block level."
    ],
    [
      "Poison-generating instruction used in a non-branching context",
      "A test case where `OpI` with `nuw`/`nsw` is used in a `icmp`, which is used in a `select` (so no UB if poison). Another `icmp` in the same block or a dominated block is simplified based on the fact from `OpI`.",
      "If `OpI` is `add nuw x, 1`, and it's used in `Cmp`, and `Cmp` is used in a `select`, then `OpI` being poison just makes the `select` result poison. It doesn't trigger UB. But the patch adds the fact `OpI >= UMin` at `OpI->getParent()`. This fact is then used to simplify other checks in `OpI->getParent()` or dominated blocks. If another check `x < 10` is simplified based on this fact, it might be miscompiled because `OpI` being poison doesn't guarantee `x` satisfies the `nuw` condition. The fact is only valid if `OpI` is guaranteed to not be poison, or if the simplified check is only used when `OpI` is not poison. But the patch adds the fact unconditionally for all dominated checks!",
      "The pass might incorrectly simplify a comparison based on a fact derived from an instruction that is not guaranteed to execute, because the fact is added at the instruction's parent block, which might not dominate the check, or the check might be before the instruction."
    ],
    [
      "Fact from instruction in a dominating block used in a dominated block",
      "A test case with `OpI` in block A, used in a `select` in block A. Block B is dominated by A and contains a check that can be simplified if `OpI`'s `nuw`/`nsw` flags are true.",
      "Similar to the above, if `OpI` is in block A, and it's used in a `icmp` in block A, and the `icmp` is used in a `select`. Block B is dominated by A. A check in block B is simplified using the fact from `OpI`. If `OpI` is poison, the program might still be well-defined (e.g., the `select` result is unused or doesn't cause UB). But the check in block B was simplified assuming `OpI` is not poison. This leads to a miscompilation.",
      "The pass might incorrectly simplify a comparison based on a fact derived from an instruction that is in a different block, where the instruction's poison is not observed."
    ]
  ],
  "thoughts": "
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 1: Prioritize specific tools.
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 2: List related tools.

1.  **Fix Understanding**:
    The patch modifies `ConstraintElimination` to use `computeConstantRange` to extract facts from the operands of comparisons.
    Specifically, for any `ICmpInst` that is not an equality comparison, it looks at its operands (`OpI`) and the operands of those operands (`SubI`).
    For each such instruction, it calls `computeConstantRange` (with `UseInstrInfo=true`, which means it will use `nuw`/`nsw` flags, `assume`s, etc.).
    If the computed constant range is not full or empty, it extracts the unsigned minimum and maximum.
    It then adds `ICMP_UGE V, UMin` and `ICMP_ULE V, UMax` as `ConditionFact`s to the constraint system.
    Crucially, these facts are added at `DT.getNode(OpI->getParent())` (or `SubI->getParent()`).

2.  **Formal Semantic Modeling**:
    Before the fix: `ConstraintElimination` only extracted facts from branches, assumes, and some specific intrinsics.
    After the fix: It also extracts facts from the *results* of instructions that feed into comparisons.
    The fact added is: `V >= UMin` and `V <= UMax`, where `UMin` and `UMax` are derived from `computeConstantRange(V)`.
    This fact is added at the dominator tree node of `V->getParent()`.
    Because it's added as a `ConditionFact`, `stable_sort` will place it *before* any `InstCheck` or `UseCheck` in the same basic block.

3.  **Assumptions Identified**:
    - **Assumption 1**: The constant range computed by `computeConstantRange` is valid for all executions of the block `V->getParent()`.
      - *Violation*: `computeConstantRange` with `UseInstrInfo=true` uses poison-generating flags like `nuw` and `nsw`. If `V` is `add nuw x, y`, its range assumes no overflow. If overflow occurs, `V` is poison.
      - If `V` is poison, the fact `V >= UMin` is technically evaluating poison, which is poison. But `ConstraintElimination` uses this fact to constrain `x` and `y`!
      - Wait, `ConstraintElimination` decomposes `V >= UMin`. If `V` is `add nuw x, y`, it decomposes to `x + y >= UMin`. This constrains `x` and `y` as if the addition did not overflow.
      - Is it valid to assume the addition did not overflow for *other* instructions in the same block or dominated blocks?
      - NO! If the addition overflows, `V` is poison. If `V` is only used in a `select` or a `phi` and never causes UB, the program is well-defined. But we used the assumption that it didn't overflow to simplify *another* check. This is a miscompilation!
    - **Assumption 2**: The fact is valid for all instructions in `V->getParent()`.
      - *Violation*: Even if `V` *does* cause UB when it's poison (e.g., it's used in a branch), the UB only happens *after* `V` is executed.
      - If there is a check *before* `V` in the same block, we cannot use the fact from `V` to simplify the check, because the program might exit or loop before reaching `V`.
      - Since `ConditionFact`s are sorted before checks in the same block, a fact from `V` at the end of the block will be used to simplify a check at the beginning of the block.

4.  **Potential Cases to Break Assumptions**:
    - **Case 1**: `V` is an instruction with `nuw`/`nsw` flags. It is used in a comparison, which is used in a `select` (so no UB if poison). Another comparison in the same block (or a dominated block) checks a condition that can be simplified if `V`'s `nuw`/`nsw` holds. The pass will simplify the second comparison, which is wrong if `V` actually overflows.
    - **Case 2**: `V` is an instruction with `nuw`/`nsw` flags. It is used in a branch (so UB if poison). However, there is another comparison *before* `V` in the same block. The pass simplifies the earlier comparison using the fact from `V`. This is wrong if the block can exit (e.g., via a throwing call or an earlier branch) before reaching `V`.

5.  **Test Strategies**:
    - Strategy 1: Poison-generating instruction used in a non-branching context.
    - Strategy 2: Fact from instruction used to simplify a check before it in the same block.
    - Strategy 3: Fact from instruction in a dominating block used in a dominated block, where the instruction's poison is not observed.
    - Strategy 4: Fact from `SubI` (operand of operand) which is poison-generating and not guaranteed to be observed.
    - Strategy 5: `computeConstantRange` using `assume` that is after the check. (Wait, `computeConstantRange` uses `CtxI`? The patch calls `computeConstantRange(V, false, true)`. It does *not* pass `CtxI` or `AC` or `DT`. So it only uses context-independent facts, like `nuw`/`nsw` flags on the instruction itself, or range metadata). Range metadata on `V` has the same poison semantics: if the value is outside the range, it's poison. So range metadata can also be used to trigger this bug."
}

## Your Task for Phase 2

In this phase, you will use the `tests_manager` tool to retrieve existing test cases, apply your proposed mutations, and verify them. You must ensure that **every test case** managed by the `tests_manager` is processed and marked as tested.

Follow this structured workflow:

### Step 1: Retrieve and Prioritize Test Cases
- Use the `tests_manager` tool with the `list` action to see all available test cases and their current status.
- Use the `tests_manager` tool with the `get` action to retrieve the full details of an untested test case.
- Due to context length and round limit, you should prioritize test cases that are more relevant to the identified issues.

### Step 2: Understand the Test Case
- Analyze the selected test case to understand its structure, input characteristics, and what it is testing.
- Call the `verify` tool to see how the LLVM IR code in the test case is transformed by the optimization pass. This can help you understand the semantics of the test case and how it relates to the fix.

### Step 3: Apply Mutations
For the selected test case, apply mutations according to your proposed test strategies:
- **Focus**: Ensure the mutations focus on exposing the potential issues identified in Phase 1.
- **Format**: Provide each mutated test case in a separate ```llvm ... ``` code block.
- **Coverage**: **CRITICAL**: You must cover all the proposed test strategies across different test cases to ensure comprehensive testing of potential issues.

### Step 4: Verify and Test
- **Verify Tool**: Use the `verify` tool to submit the generated test cases along with your reasoning. This uses `alive2` to check if the test cases expose any issues with the fix.
- **Difftest Tool**: Use the `difftest` tool to execute the original and optimized LLVM IR (transformed by `opt`) with specific input values to check for execution differences.

### Step 5: Analyze Results and Refine
- If the `verify` or `difftest` fails to find issues, refine the test case only if you believe unexplored potential issues remain. Otherwise, move on to other strategies or test cases. Check **Guidelines for Refining Test Cases** below for suggestions on how to refine test cases based on verification results.

### Step 6: Mark as Tested
- Once you have fully explored and verified a test case, use the `tests_manager` tool with the `mark_tested` action to mark it as completed.
- **CRITICAL**: You must repeat this process until the `tests_manager` confirms that **all** test cases have been tested.

Repeat the above steps for each test case until all test cases in the `tests_manager` are marked as tested.

You are also allowed to generate tests from scratch or find more related tests from the LLVM test suite by using `find` or `list` tools and reading them with `read` or `grep` tools if you think the provided test cases have limited coverage of potential issues. Make sure you call `verify` and `difftest` tools to check the validity of these test cases and confirm whether the issues can be exposed by actual execution.

---

## Guidelines for Refining Test Cases

### Important Rule
You can always keep mutating the test case and verifying it until you find an issue or are confident that no issues can be found. However, make sure it is aligned with the potential issues you identified in Phase 1. Meanwhile, avoid stucking on a test case for too long. If you have tried multiple mutations and still cannot find any issues, move on to other test cases or strategies.

### Handling Verification Results
- **Failed-to-prove**:
  1. Try to reduce the test case to a smaller example that still fails. This can help isolate the specific conditions that cause the issue.
  2. Try to call `difftest` to run the test case with specific input value that you think can trigger the issue based on your analysis. This can help check if the issue can be exposed by actual execution even if it cannot be proved by alive2.
- **Alive2 errors**: Try to call `trans` to run opt with the same command arguments and adjust the test case or command arguments to fix.
- **Correct transformation**: Try to first analyze if the test strategy is correct and then decide to refine the test case or continue to generate other test cases.

### Additional Notes
- **Coverage**: If you think the provided test cases have limited coverage of potential issues, you can also find more related tests from the LLVM test suite by using `find` or `list` tools and reading them with `read` or `grep` tools.
- **Completeness**: Make sure you have at least explored all the test strategies you proposed in Phase 1, and generated multiple test cases if possible.
- **Completion**: You cannot finish Phase 2 until all tests in the `tests_manager` are marked as tested.

---

## Tools that you can use

- `findN`: Search for files in the LLVM codebase related to the component or optimization pass to understand the context of the fix and find relevant tests.
- `listN`: List files in the LLVM codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `readN`: Read the content of a file in the LLVM codebase to understand existing tests or the fix.
- `grepN`: Search for specific patterns in the codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `tests_manager`: Manage the list of test cases. You can list all tests to see their status, get the details of a specific test, or mark a test as tested. Your goal is to ensure all tests are tested.
- `trans`: Run the `opt` tool with specific arguments to see how the LLVM IR code is transformed by the optimization pass.
- `verify`: Use alive2 to verify if the transformation from original LLVM IR code to optimized LLVM IR code is correct, which can help check the validity of generated test cases in Phase 2.
- `difftest`: Use llubi to perform differential testing on the original and transformed LLVM IR code, which can help check if the generated test cases cannot be proved by alive2.
- `report`: End Phase 2 by submitting the generated test cases and their verification results.

name: difftest
arguments:
{"action":"test","args":"-passes=constraint-elimination","thoughts":"
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 1: Prioritize specific tools.
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 2: List related tools.

I will use `difftest` to check if the transformation is correct when `%y` is large enough to cause overflow.
Wait, if `%y` is `4294967295` (`-1`), `%off = add nuw i32 %y, 9` will overflow and produce poison.
If `%off` is poison, `%cmp` is poison.
Then `%res = select i1 poison, i1 %r, i1 false` is poison.
Returning poison is UB in C, but in LLVM IR it just returns poison.
Wait, if it returns poison, Alive2 considers it correct if the optimized version also returns poison or a more defined value.
In the optimized version, it returns `%cmp`, which is poison. So both return poison.
To make it a miscompilation, the original program must return a well-defined value, but the optimized program returns a different well-defined value or poison.
How can we make the original program return a well-defined value even if `%off` is poison?
If `%cmp` is poison, `select i1 %cmp, ...` is poison.
What if we use `freeze`?
If we freeze `%cmp`, it becomes a non-deterministic boolean.
If it's true, it returns `%r`. If it's false, it returns `false`.
If `%y` is `-1`, `%w = add nuw i32 0, -1` is `-1`.
`%r = icmp ugt i32 10, -1` is `false`.
So if `%cmp` is frozen to true, it returns `false`. If frozen to false, it returns `false`.
So the original program always returns `false`!
But the optimized program returns `freeze(%cmp)`. Which can be true!
Let's test this!","test_index":0,"orig_ir":"```llvm
define i1 @test_poison_not_observed3(i32 range(i32 0, 10) %x, i32 noundef %y, i32 noundef %z) {
entry:

  %r = icmp ugt i32 %z, %w
  %off = add nuw i32 %y, 9
  %cmp = icmp ugt i32 %z, %off
  %res = select i1 %cmp, i1 %r, i1 false
  ret i1 %res
}
```","covered_strategy":"Poison-generating instruction used for constant range fact","call_instr":"call i1 @test_poison_not_observed3(i32 0, i32 4294967295, i32 10)"}

So if `%cmp` is frozen to true, it returns `false`. If frozen to false, it returns `false`.
So the original program always returns `false`!

entry:
  %w = add nuw i32 %x, %y
  %r = icmp ugt i32 %z, %w
  br i1 %r, label %then, label %else

then:
  %off = add nuw i32 %y, 9
  %cmp = icmp ugt i32 %z, %off
  ret i1 %cmp

else:
  ret i1 false
}
```","thoughts":"
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 1: Prioritize specific tools.
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 2: List related tools.

Let's use `difftest` to check if `test_check_before_poison2` triggers a miscompilation.
Wait, in `test_check_before_poison2`, the optimized IR is exactly the same as the original IR.
Why? Because `%off` is in the `then` block, and `%r` is in the `entry` block.
The fact from `%off` is added at `DT.getNode(then)`.
So it's only available for checks dominated by `then`.
`%r` is in `entry`, which is not dominated by `then`.
So the fact from `%off` is NOT used to simplify `%r`.
This means my assumption that facts are added at the block level and used for all checks in the block is correct, but if the instruction is in a *different* block, it's only used for dominated blocks.
What if `%off` is in the *same* block as `%r`, but *after* `%r`?
Let's look at `test_check_before_poison3`.
In `test_check_before_poison3`, `%off` is before `%r`.
Wait, in `test_check_before_poison3`, `%off` is in `entry`, `%r` is in `then`.
`entry` dominates `then`. So the fact from `%off` is used to simplify `%r`.
And Alive2 says it's correct!
Why? Because if `%off` is poison, `%cmp` is poison, and `br i1 %cmp` is UB.
So if we reach `then`, `%off` must not be poison.
So the fact is valid!

CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 1: Prioritize specific tools.
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 2: List related tools.

Wait, Alive2 says `test_check_before_poison_throw` is correct!
Why?
Because if `throw()` throws, the function doesn't return, so the return value doesn't matter.
If `throw()` returns, then `%off` is executed.
If `%off` is poison, `%cmp` is poison, `%res` is poison.
The optimized version returns `%cmp`, which is poison.
So both return poison!
Ah! The issue is that the original program returns poison, and the optimized program returns poison.
To make it a miscompilation, the original program must return a specific value, and the optimized program must return a different specific value.
How can we make the original program return a specific value?
If we use `freeze` on `%off`? No, `ConstraintElimination` won't extract facts from `freeze`.
What if we use the fact to simplify a check that is *before* the poison-generating instruction, and the poison-generating instruction is *not* executed?
Wait, if the poison-generating instruction is not executed, it doesn't produce poison!
If it doesn't produce poison, how can it be a miscompilation?
If `%off` is not executed, it doesn't produce poison.
But `ConstraintElimination` extracts the fact from `%off` and uses it to simplify `%r`!
If `%r` is simplified to `true`, but it should be `false`, then the program behaves incorrectly *before* `throw()`!
How can we observe the incorrect behavior of `%r` before `throw()`?
We can pass `%r` to `throw()`!

Wait, `test_load_range5` is optimized to `ret i1 %cmp`.
If `%p` is null, `load` triggers UB.

Then `%v` is poison.
If `%v` is poison, `%cmp` is poison.
`%res = select i1 poison, i1 %r, i1 false` is poison.
The optimized version returns `%cmp`, which is poison.
So both return poison!
Alive2 says it's correct because both return poison.
To make it a miscompilation, we need the original program to return a specific value, and the optimized program to return a different specific value.
How can we make the original program return a specific value when `%v` is poison?
If we freeze `%v`?
If we freeze `%v`, `ConstraintElimination` won't extract facts from it, because `freeze` doesn't have `!range` metadata.
Wait, what if we freeze `%cmp`?
If we freeze `%cmp`, it becomes a non-deterministic boolean.
If it's true, it returns `%r`. If it's false, it returns `false`.
If `%z` is `0`, `%r` is `false`.
So if `%cmp` is frozen to true, it returns `false`. If frozen to false, it returns `false`.
So the original program always returns `false`!
But the optimized program returns `freeze(%cmp)`. Which can be true!
